title: Skin Conditions — Eczema, Fungal Infections, and Heat Rash
topic: Skin Conditions — Eczema, Fungal Infections, and Heat Rash

content.overview:
Skin diseases are among the most common reasons for outpatient visits in India. The hot, humid climate promotes fungal and bacterial infections, while air pollution, hard water, sweating, and increased use of synthetic clothing worsen inflammatory skin conditions like eczema. Heat rash is extremely common across India in summer months. While most common skin conditions are not life-threatening, they significantly affect quality of life, and stigma around visible skin conditions is a significant public health issue. Early and correct diagnosis matters, as many skin conditions look similar and require different treatments.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
This information is for health awareness only. Do not self-diagnose skin conditions or apply any cream — especially steroid-containing creams — without a qualified doctor's prescription. Many skin conditions look similar but require different treatments; incorrect treatment can worsen the condition or cause lasting skin damage.
types: Definition: A chronic, inflammatory skin condition characterised by dry, itchy, and inflamed skin. It is part of the "atopic triad" — often co-exists with asthma and allergic rhinitis (hay fever). It is not contagious.
Symptoms:
Dry, scaly, or rough skin
Intense itching (often worse at night)
Red to brownish-grey patches
Small, raised bumps that may weep fluid when scratched
Thickened, cracked, or scaly skin from repeated scratching
Raw, swollen skin from scratching
Common sites:
Infants: Face, cheeks, scalp, outer surfaces of limbs
Children and adults: Behind knees, inside elbows, wrists, hands, feet, ankles, neck
Triggers:
Dry skin (low humidity, air conditioning, hot showers)
Irritants: Soaps, detergents, shampoos, certain fabrics (synthetic, wool)
Allergens: Dust mites, pet dander, pollen, certain foods (in some children)
Sweat (especially in India's hot and humid climate)
Stress
Skin infections
Hard water
Prevention and Management:
Moisturise liberally and frequently (emollients/moisturisers) — cornerstone of management; apply immediately after bathing
Use mild, fragrance-free soap or soap-free cleansers
Bathe in lukewarm (not hot) water; pat dry gently
Wear soft, loose cotton clothing
Keep nails short to prevent damage from scratching
Identify and avoid personal triggers
Use prescribed topical steroids or non-steroid immunomodulators during flares — do not self-medicate
Antihistamines for itch relief (doctor advised)

Overview: Fungal skin infections (tinea or ringworm infections) are extremely prevalent in India, dramatically increasing since around 2017–2018 due to widespread misuse of topical steroid-antifungal-antibiotic combination creams (sold over-the-counter). India is facing an epidemic of chronic, recalcitrant dermatophytosis — difficult-to-treat ringworm — recognised as a national dermatological crisis.
Types by Body Site:
Tinea corporis: Ringworm of the body — ring-shaped, scaly, itchy patches
Tinea cruris (Jock itch): Groin area; ring-shaped or irregular red, itchy patches
Tinea capitis: Scalp ringworm; scaly patches, hair loss; common in children
Tinea pedis (Athlete's foot): Scaling, itching, cracking between toes and sole of feet
Tinea unguium (Onychomycosis): Nail fungal infection; yellow, thick, brittle nails
Tinea versicolor (Pityriasis versicolor): Caused by Malassezia yeast; causes hypo/hyperpigmented (lighter or darker) patches on trunk and arms; very common in India's humid climate
Symptoms of Tinea (Ringworm):
Ring-shaped or circular reddish patch with clear centre
Itching (often intense)
Scaly, flaky border
May blister or ooze
Enlarging rings over time
CRITICAL — The Steroid Cream Problem in India:
Many branded creams sold in Indian medical shops combine a steroid (e.g. clobetasol) + antifungal + antibiotic (e.g. Panderm, Betnovate-C, Topivate, Quadriderm)
These are PRESCRIPTION-ONLY but widely sold OTC and self-applied for any rash
Initial use suppresses redness/itching (steroid effect), appearing to improve, but the fungus survives — infection spreads and becomes chronic and treatment-resistant
Long-term steroid use causes skin thinning, stretch marks, acne, and systemic steroid absorption
DO NOT use combination steroid-antifungal creams without a dermatologist's prescription
Prevention:
Keep skin dry, especially in skin folds (groin, underarms, between toes)
Wear loose, breathable cotton underwear and socks
Change sweaty clothes promptly
Avoid sharing towels, footwear, combs
Wear footwear in public showers/changing rooms
Treat household contacts and pets if they have ringworm
Treatment (Awareness Only — Consult Doctor):
Topical antifungals (clotrimazole, miconazole, terbinafine): For limited mild infections
Oral antifungals (terbinafine, itraconazole, fluconazole): For extensive, recalcitrant, or nail fungal infections — must be prescribed by a doctor
Treatment duration matters — fungal infections must be treated for the full prescribed duration even if symptoms resolve

Definition: Heat rash (prickly heat) occurs when sweat ducts become blocked and sweat is trapped under the skin. Extremely common in India, especially in infants, young children, and adults in hot and humid conditions.
Types:
Miliaria crystallina: Superficial; tiny, clear blisters; no inflammation; least symptomatic
Miliaria rubra (Prickly Heat): Most common; small red bumps with intense prickling or itching; forms in skin folds and clothing-covered areas
Miliaria profunda: Deeper, flesh-coloured bumps; less common; more uncomfortable
Common Sites:
Neck, chest, back, armpits, groin, and under breasts
Any area where skin rubs against skin or clothing
Symptoms:
Clusters of small red bumps or blisters
Prickling, stinging, or itchy sensation
Mild inflammation (redness, warmth)
May develop secondary infection if scratched
Prevention and Management:
Wear loose, lightweight, breathable cotton clothing
Stay in cool or well-ventilated environments (fan, air conditioning if available)
Shower with cool water after sweating
Keep skin dry; use talcum powder on susceptible areas (use sparingly on infants)
Avoid thick creams or ointments in affected areas (trap heat and sweat)
Calamine lotion soothes symptoms
Most heat rash resolves within days when the skin cools down
See a doctor if infected (pustules, increasing pain, fever)
Scabies:
Highly contagious skin infestation caused by a microscopic mite (Sarcoptes scabiei)
Intense itching, especially at night; small burrows and papules between fingers, wrists, elbows, waist, buttocks, genitals
Spreads through close skin-to-skin contact (common in overcrowded settings)
Requires topical permethrin or oral ivermectin — prescribed by doctor; treat all household contacts simultaneously
Psoriasis:
Chronic autoimmune skin condition causing rapid build-up of skin cells; forms scales and red patches that can be itchy and painful
Not contagious; not curable but manageable
Triggers: Stress, infections, injury, certain medications
Treatment: Topical steroids, moisturisers, phototherapy, systemic therapy — doctor managed
Urticaria (Hives):
Raised, itchy welts on the skin; can appear anywhere; allergic or non-allergic triggers
Acute urticaria: Resolves within 6 weeks; antihistamines help
Chronic urticaria: Persists >6 weeks; requires medical evaluation

treatment_awareness_only: Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Any skin condition that doesn't improve within 2 weeks
Rash spreading rapidly or becoming more painful
Signs of skin infection (increasing pain, pus, swelling, fever)
Itching severe enough to disrupt sleep
Rash in a child with fever
Do not apply combination steroid-antifungal creams without prescription
Nail fungal infection (often requires prolonged oral treatment — self-treatment is usually ineffective)
india_specific_context: India faces a national epidemic of drug-resistant, chronic dermatophytosis linked to OTC steroid combination cream misuse
IADVL (Indian Association of Dermatologists, Venereologists and Leprologists) has issued national guidelines and campaigns on appropriate antifungal use
Free dermatology OPDs at government medical college hospitals and district hospitals
Tele-dermatology services increasingly available via eSanjeevani (esanjeevani.mohfw.gov.in)
